sections.title: Fats
sections.narration: 0.1g of fat. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for a vegetable, you want real micronutrient value, not just green vibes.
sections.subtitleText: 0.1g of fat. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for a vegetable, you want real micronutrient value, not just green vibes.

sections.title: Carbs
sections.narration: 5.8g of carbs. glycemic index, fibre, and sugar are doing most of the work. for vegetables, low downside is useful, but there still needs to be real payoff.

sections.title: Proteins

sections.title: Vitamins

sections.title: Pros
sections.narration: the upsides first: makes meals feel more balanced. the category has real value beyond single-nutrient stats. easy to build meals around. a practical plus because good foods need realistic use cases. helps add volume without much calorie cost. that makes vegetables easier to use often.

sections.title: Cons
sections.narration: now the downsides: freshness and prep can matter. practical friction is still a real downside. rarely carries a meal by itself. that is normal here, but still part of the tradeoff. usually needs pairing to make a full meal. most vegetables are not complete by themselves.

closing.summary: Cabbage has a genuinely strong overall case. the practical downsides are real, but they do not outweigh the upside. best when you want low downside and easy micronutrient support.
